saves-menu.title: Your saves
saves-menu.rename-content: You are about to rename save:
saves-menu.delete-content: Are you sure, you want to delete save:

objectives.teacher: Find and talk with the teacher.

lessons.dial1-1: To add a library to the program we use <color=yellow>#include<library></color>
lessons.dial1-2: Everything inside the <color=yellow>main</color> function will be executed by the program after compilation
lessons.dial1-3: <color=yellow>std::cout << "hello world"</color> command, makes the text inside double-quotes appear on the screen
lessons.dial1-4: At the end of every line, with some exeptions, there needs to be a semicolon
lessons.dial1-5: It's not necessary to use <color=yellow>std::</color> when you add <color=yellow>using namespace std</color> at the very top of your code
lessons.dial2-1: You don't have to use <color=yellow>std::</color>, if you add <color=yellow>using namespace std</color> at the top of your code
lessons.dial3-1: Using the program above you can assign values to two variables and display their sum
lessons.dial3-2: With the help of variables, we can store some data, which can be used later in the program
lessons.dial3-3: There are multiple variable types, storing diffrent types of data:
lessons.dial3-8: <color=yellow>bool</color> stores a logical data - true or false
lessons.dial4-1: <color=yellow>If</color> statement allows to execute some code under a certain condition, which we put inside the brackets
lessons.dial4-2: The code above shows <color=yellow>x > 0</color>, if value of variable x is greater that zero
lessons.dialCom-1: By writing double slash sign "//" we make program ignore all text in that line after these signs
lessons.dialCom-2: By writing two slash signs, and two star signs between them "/* */", we make program ignore all text between the star signs
lessons.dialCom-3: The advantage of the second method is that a comment can go on for multiple lines, unlike the first method
lessons.dialCom-4: Comments are used to describe what a certain piece of code does, or for writing some information for other programmers reading our code
lessons.dial5-1: In c++ as well as in many other programming languages, you can use mathematical operators with variables
lessons.dial5-2: As you can see in examples above, there are many ways to use them:
lessons.dial5-8: <color=yellow>F</color> - Remainder of division of x and y
lessons.dial6-1: When using <color=yellow>integers</color> for operations such as division, the result is often a real number which needs <color=yellow>float</color>
lessons.dial6-2: Unfortunately this means that when using <color=yellow>ints</color> you often have to change its type to <color=yellow>floating point number</color>
lessons.dial6-3: Otherwise calculations will produce incorrect results
lessons.dial6-4: As you can see in example <color=yellow>A</color>, the result is <color=yellow>1</color> because c++ automaticaly thinks that operations with two ints should produce an <color=yellow>int</color>
lessons.dial6-5: Using method <color=yellow>B</color> is the best for int divisions because it forces c++ to treat these numbers as <color=yellow>floats</color>
lessons.dial6-6: But method <color=yellow>C</color> also works, thanks to multiplying numbers by <color=yellow>1.0</color> which is a <color=yellow>float</color>
lessons.dial6-7: As you can see in examples <color=yellow>D</color> and <color=yellow>E</color> it also works with changing <color=yellow>floats</color> to <color=yellow>ints</color>
lessons.dial6-8: Going back to example <color=yellow>C</color> you can see that it is possible to use <color=yellow>brackets</color> in calculations, just like in real arithmetics
lessons.dial7-1: <color=yellow>Loop</color> is expression, which allows us to repeat some action
lessons.dial7-2: <color=yellow>for</color> loop is the simplest type of loop
lessons.dial7-3: We define it in following way:
lessons.dial7-5: Everthing in braces will be executed while condition is true
lessons.dial8-0: Type which allows us to store logical data is <color=yellow>bool</color>
lessons.dial8-1: In logical operations <color=yellow>1</color> stands for <color=yellow>true</color>, and <color=yellow>0</color> - <color=yellow>false</color>
lessons.dial8-2: Example <color=yellow>A</color> will display <color=yellow>1</color>(true), because value of x is equal to 3
lessons.dial8-3: On the other hand, example <color=yellow>B</color> will display <color=yellow>0</color>, because 3 isn't equal to 2
lessons.dial8-4: Example <color=yellow>C</color> will display <color=yellow>1</color>, because value of x (3) is greater than 2
lessons.dial8-5: Example <color=yellow>D</color> will display <color=yellow>1</color> as well, because 3 is less or equal to 3
lessons.dial8-6: In example <color=yellow>E</color> the <color=yellow>AND</color> operator is being used(double & mark). It returns 1 if <color=yellow>BOTH</color> operations returns true
lessons.dial8-7: Example <color=yellow>E</color> will display <color=yellow>1</color>, because <color=yellow>BOTH</color> operations return true
lessons.dial8-8: In example <color=yellow>F</color> the <color=yellow>OR</color> operator is being used(double | mark). It returs true <color=yellow>if one of operations</color> is true
lessons.dial8-9: In examples <color=yellow>F</color> and <color=yellow>G</color> used conversion from <color=yellow>int</color> to <color=yellow>bool</color>
lessons.dial8-10: Example <color=yellow>F</color> will return <color=yellow>0</color>, and <color=yellow>E</color> will return <color=yellow>1</color> according to the rules of logic
lessons.dial9-1: <color=yellow>Array</color>, is a list of variables of a certain type , which allows to store <color=yellow>multiple values</color> in one variable
lessons.dial9-3: When we define array without specifying number of elements, we have to assign values to it
lessons.dial9-4: We do it the following way: <color=yellow>type name[] = { element1, element2 }</color>
lessons.dial9-5: When we don't want to define content of the array right away, we have to set <color=yellow>element count</color>
lessons.dial9-6: To reference a specific element in array we have to pass it's index to the array(counting starts from zero!)
lessons.dial9-8: To get a size of the array, we need to calculate it by ourself
lessons.dial9-10: We can make it more universal, by passing the first element of the array instead of array type in sizeof() function
lessons.dial9-12: To iterate through the array we use previously learned <color=yellow>loops</color>
lessons.dial9-13: Example of iteration can be seen in the code above
lessons.dial9-14: By referencing element of the array we are able to manipulate and display them
lessons.dial9-15: Above is an example of manipulating element values and displaying them
lessons.dial9-16: <color=yellow>Arrays</color> can have diffrent types, for example the array above is a text type - (<color=yellow>string</color>)
lessons.dial9-17: Loop above displays all values from array seperated with space

messages.too-young: You are too young to drive a car!
messages.leave-during-lesson: You can't leave class during lesson.
messages.leave-during-python: I think I am supposed to do something here.
